coredata.dc:title: Tuning the carboxyl content of carbon quantum dots by an electrochemical exfoliation method to enhance Fenton-like reactions for contaminants removal in a wide pH range
coredata.dc:creator: Li, Yibo
Liang, Jianxing
Li, Jingdong
Liao, Liyan
Guo, Jiaxin
Jia, Jinping
coredata.dc:description: The complexation of CQDs with Fe(III) through -COO-Fe bond in the CQDs-Fe(III)/H2O2 system can accelerate the Fe(III)/Fe(II) cycle and broaden the pH range for application. However, the yield and carboxyl content of CQDs prepared by conventional electrochemical exfoliation were limited, which hampered the application of CQDs and the enhancement of CQDs Fenton-like process. Hence, in this study, carboxyl-rich and high-yield CQDs were successfully prepared by electrochemical exfoliation of graphite rods in hydrogen peroxide (H2O2) solution. The results suggested that the yield and carboxyl content of the optimal CQDs (CQD@5) were 1.49 and 2.01 times those of CQDs prepared by the conventional electrochemical exfoliation method (CQD@0), respectively. Subsequently, the CQDs were applied in CQDs-Fe(III)/H2O2 systems for phenol degradation. Due to 1.73 times the carboxyl of CQD@ 5 complexing with Fe(III) than that of CQD@ 0, the kinetic constant and removal of the CQD@ 5-Fe(III)/H2O2 system for phenol degradation were 2.36 and 1.44 times those of the CQD@ 0-Fe(III)/H2O2 system at pH= 6, respectively. Under the same condition, Fe(III)/H2O2 and Fe(II)/H2O2 showed negligible degradation performance. Furthermore, the removals of phenol in the CQD@ 5-Fe(III)/H2O2 system were 1.08–7.77 times those of the CQD@ 0-Fe(III)/H2O2 system at pH= 4–8, respectively. Finally, the effect of Fe(III), CQDs, and H2O2 dosages on the phenol degradation, reusability, and universality of CQD@ 5-Fe(III)/H2O2 were investigated individually. In summary, this work provides a new method for tuning the CQDs yield and carboxyl content, which enhances pollutant degradation in CQDs-Fe(III)/H2O2 systems at a wide pH range.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon quantum dots
Surface regulation
Carboxyl
Fenton-like reaction

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Defect level and functional groups of CQDs
CQDs-Fe(III)/H2O2 systems for the degradation of pollutants
The mechanics for the degradation-performance improvement
The effect of the reaction parameter

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: National Natural Science Foundation of China
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was financially supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Project No. 21737002 ). The authors would like to thank Mr. Kan Li for guiding the thesis ideas and Ms. Yan Zhu for her help in the experiments.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Yield of CQDs. (b) TEM and size distribution. (c) Raman spectra of CQDs. (d) C1s high-resolution XPS spectra of CQDs. (e) oxygen-containing functional groups of CQDs (f) pK a of CQDs.

(a) UV–vis spectrum of CQD@ 5-Fe(III). (b) C1s (yellow) and O 1 s (blue) high-resolution XPS spectra of CQD@ 5 (left) and CQD@ 5-Fe(III) (right) (c) FT-IR spectra of CQD@ 5-Fe(III). (d) Carboxyl content of CQD@ 5 and CQD@ 5-Fe(III).

(a) The degradation of phenol by CQDs-Fe(III)/H2O2 systems. (b) Comparison of phenol degradation in different systems. Reaction conditions: [CQDs]= 22 mg/L, [Fe(III)]= 12 μM, [H2O2]= 6.4 mM, [phenol]= 10 mg/L, pH= 6, Temperature= 25 ℃.

(a) ESR spectra of DMPO-·OH in CQDs-Fe(III)/H2O2 systems. (b) XPS of Fe in CQDs-Fe(III)/H2O2 systems (c) Fe(II)/Fe(Total) ratio in CQDs-Fe(III)/H2O2 systems. (d) Liner fit of Δ -COOH to Δ Binding Energy of Fe in CQDs-Fe(III)/H2O2 systems.
The removal of phenol in different systems at pH= 4–9. Reaction conditions: [CQDs]= 22 mg/L, [Fe(III)]= [Fe(II)]= 12 μM, [H2O2]= 6.4 mM, [phenol]= 10 mg/L, reaction time = 60 min, temperature= 25 ℃.
Effect of different amount of iron ion (a), CQD@ 5 (b), and H2O2 (c) on phenol degradation.

(a) Reusability of CQD@ 5-Fe(III)/H2O2 system for phenol degradation in pure water. (b) The degradation of 4-CP, Phenol, and BPA by different systems. Reaction conditions: [4-CP]= 13.7 mg/L, [BPA]= 20 mg/L, [Phenol]= 10 mg/L, [Fe(III)]= 12 μM, [H2O2]= 6.4 mM, T = 25 ℃, reaction time= 60 min, initial solution pH= 6.0.

head.ce:title: Tuning the carboxyl content of carbon quantum dots by an electrochemical exfoliation method to enhance Fenton-like reactions for contaminants removal in a wide pH range

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Environmental Science and Engineering, Shanghai Jiao Tong University, 800 Dong Chuan Road, Shanghai 200240, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Environmental Science and Engineering, Shanghai Jiao Tong University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Environmental Science and Engineering, Shanghai Jiao Tong University, 800 Dong Chuan Road, Shanghai, 200240, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The complexation of CQDs with Fe(III) through -COO-Fe bond in the CQDs-Fe(III)/H2O2 system can accelerate the Fe(III)/Fe(II) cycle and broaden the pH range for application. However, the yield and carboxyl content of CQDs prepared by conventional electrochemical exfoliation were limited, which hampered the application of CQDs and the enhancement of CQDs Fenton-like process. Hence, in this study, carboxyl-rich and high-yield CQDs were successfully prepared by electrochemical exfoliation of graphite rods in hydrogen peroxide (H2O2) solution. The results suggested that the yield and carboxyl content of the optimal CQDs (CQD@5) were 1.49 and 2.01 times those of CQDs prepared by the conventional electrochemical exfoliation method (CQD@0), respectively. Subsequently, the CQDs were applied in CQDs-Fe(III)/H2O2 systems for phenol degradation. Due to 1.73 times the carboxyl of CQD@ 5 complexing with Fe(III) than that of CQD@ 0, the kinetic constant and removal of the CQD@ 5-Fe(III)/H2O2 system for phenol degradation were 2.36 and 1.44 times those of the CQD@ 0-Fe(III)/H2O2 system at pH= 6, respectively. Under the same condition, Fe(III)/H2O2 and Fe(II)/H2O2 showed negligible degradation performance. Furthermore, the removals of phenol in the CQD@ 5-Fe(III)/H2O2 system were 1.08–7.77 times those of the CQD@ 0-Fe(III)/H2O2 system at pH= 4–8, respectively. Finally, the effect of Fe(III), CQDs, and H2O2 dosages on the phenol degradation, reusability, and universality of CQD@ 5-Fe(III)/H2O2 were investigated individually. In summary, this work provides a new method for tuning the CQDs yield and carboxyl content, which enhances pollutant degradation in CQDs-Fe(III)/H2O2 systems at a wide pH range.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para:
As society develops, water pollution has become a major environmental issue worldwide[1]. For now, biodegradation, physical separation, and advanced oxidation techniques are three widely used methods for wastewater treatment [2,3]. Biodegradation and physical separation are limited by their efficiency and dependence on raw water quality. As a representative of advanced oxidation technology, Fenton technology is widely applied for wastewater treatment due to its excellent oxidation performance and minimal environmental impact [1,4]. Catalyzed by Fe(II), H2O2 decomposes into·OH effectively in the Fenton process (Eqs. 1, 2), which could oxidize pollutants non-selectively. However, the conventional Fenton process suffers from three drawbacks: (1) A narrow pH range for applications, usually 2.0–3.5 [5]; (2) A sluggish Fe(III)/Fe(II) cycle; and (3) Low utilization of hydrogen peroxide.
(1) Fe(II) + H2O2 → Fe(III) +·OH + OH- k=63 M−1·s−1
(2) Fe(III) + H2O2 → Fe(II) + H+ + HO2·k=0·01–0·002 M−1·s−1

To address these issues, heterogeneous catalysts, such as CuS-Fe(III)[6], Ti3C2-Fe(III)[7], and CuMgFe-B(OH)4 [8] were proposed to regulate the leaching of iron ions and slow down the formation of iron sludge. On the other hand, NH2OH[9,10], EDTA[11], and gallic acid[12] were used as chelating agents to accelerate the Fe(III)/Fe(II) cycle in homogeneous Fenton-like systems under higher pH conditions. However, heterogeneous catalysts are suppressed by: (1) Restricted active site exposure; (2) Poor mass transfer efficiency; and (3) Ineffective oxidant utilization[13]. The use of ligands in homogeneous catalytic systems to speed up the Fe(III)/Fe(II) cycle is also troublesome for secondary pollution and catalyst recovery.

Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) with particle sizes less than 10 nm are characterized by superior photochemical properties [14,15], little toxicity[16–18], and abundant surface functional groups[19,20], which exhibit great promise for detection, energy, and medicine[21]. In recent years, CQDs have been combined with Cu-Al2O3 [22], zero-valent iron nanoparticles[15], α-FeOOH[23], Schwertmannites[24], and CuO/g-C3N4 [25] as heterogenous catalysts to activate H2O2 for pollutant degradation. Their excellent contaminant degradation performances have demonstrated the great potential of CQDs for wastewater treatment. However, these catalysts are complex in preparation and suffer from the drawbacks of heterogeneous catalysts mentioned above.

Different from the studies above that used CQDs to build a solid catalyst, many studies suggest that CQDs, uniformly dispersed in water, can bond with metal ions, such as iron[14], zinc[26], and aluminum[27]. Changes in fluorescence properties of CQDs due to complexation are used to detect and quantify the relevant ions. Additionally, homogeneous cobalt-doped carbon dot nanozymes are constructed and applied to catalyst H2O2 for tumor catalytic therapy[20]. In the field of water treatment, CQDs complexed with Fe(III) are employed as a homogeneous catalyst (CQDs-Fe(III)) to activate H2O2 for pollutant degradation in water[28,29]. CQDs that bond with Fe(III) via carboxyl bidentate coordination can efficiently accelerate the Fe(III)/Fe(II) cycle via through-bond electro-transfer pathways. Meanwhile, the strong oxidation potential of·OH is strengthened by the buffer capacity of carboxyl. Therefore, the reaction rate constant for phenol degradation in the CQDs-Fe(III)/H2O2 system can be 38-fold that of Fe(III)/H2O2 at pH= 5[29]. It is evident that the carboxyl on the surface of CQDs plays a significant role in the CQDs-Fe(III)/H2O2 system. However, the CQDs produced by the typical electrochemical exfoliation method have a low yield and a limited carboxyl content, which hampers their application and performance in a Fenton-like process. Therefore, it is essential to find an approach to improve the yield and carboxyl group content of CQDs.

The "bottom-up" methods for synthesizing CQDs, such as hydrothermal[30,31] and pyrolysis[20] of organics, are complex and difficult to prepare on a large scale. As a representative of the "top-down" method, the electrochemical exfoliation method is characterized by its simplicity of preparation and the availability of raw materials[32]. During the electrolytic preparation of CQDs using graphite rods as cathode and anode,·OH and·O from H2O or CC act as electrochemical "scissors" to break up the graphite precursors and produce nanoscale quantum dots[33]. Therefore, increasing the amount of free radicals in the system is an effective way to promote the generation of CQDs[34]. As a clean oxidant, H2O2 can be reduced to strongly oxidizing·OH on the cathode surface during electrolysis[35], which is expected to be an extra precursor for·OH to strengthen the production of CQDs. Therefore, in this work, CQDs were prepared by electrochemical exfoliation of graphite rods in hydrogen peroxide (H2O2) solution. The yield, morphology, and functional group content of different CQDs prepared with different amounts of H2O2 in the electrolyte were investigated. Then, these CQDs were employed in the CQDs-Fe(III)/H2O2 system to degrade phenol at pH= 6. Their performances were recorded, and the reason for the discrepancy in the phenol degradation performance of different systems was demonstrated. In addition, the performances for phenol degradation by different CQDs-Fe(III)/H2O2 systems at pH= 4–9 were collected. Finally, the effects of Fe(III), CQDs, and H2O2 dosage on phenol degradation, reusability, and universality were examined. In summary, this study provides a method for tuning the yield and carboxyl content of the CQDs and further promotes the application of CQDs in Fenton-like systems.

Information on the chemicals used is provided in Text S1. The CQDs were produced by electrochemical exfoliation[28,29]. Typically, two commercial graphite rods were inserted into 500 mL of electrolyte with a specific amount of H2O2. A voltage of 30 V was applied for 48 h. Then, the solution was filtered with a PTFE filter, and the filtrate obtained was a CQDs solution. According to the concentration of H2O2 in the electrolyte at 0, 1, 5, and 10 mM, the CQDs obtained were named CQDs@ 0, CQDs@ 1, CQDs@ 5, and CQDs@ 10, respectively.

Transmission electron microscope (TEM) images were collected using the TEM of the Talos F200X G2. Raman spectra were measured by confocal micro-Raman spectroscopy of Renishow via Qontor. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was measured with the XPS of the AXIS UltraDLD. Boehm titration and Acid-base titration were carried out by a potentiometric titrator of 888 Titrando. The organic compounds were determined by the high-performance liquid chromatography of Shimadzu LC-16. Electron paramagnetic resonance (EPR) signals were recorded on the electron paramagnetic resonance spectrometer of the Bruker A300.

Typically, the CQDs-Fe(III) solution was first mixed with the contaminant solution to obtain a mixture (CQDs@5 =22 mg/L, Fe(III)= 12 μmol/L, and Phenol= 10 mg/L). The pH of the mixture was adjusted with 0.5 mol/L NaOH or 0.5 mol/L HNO3 to a specific pH condition. Subsequently, H2O2 was added to the above solution to initiate the reaction, and the temperature was maintained at 25 °C. The reaction suspension was taken at specific intervals and quenched with 100 μL of tertiary butanol. Then, the sample was filtered through a 0.22 µm PTFE membrane and analyzed by high-performance liquid chromatography. The Fenton and Fenton-like reactions were carried out the same way except for the addition of CQDs.

In a typical cycling experiment, 5.27 μmol of Fe(III) was first added to the 80 mL reaction system and stirred thoroughly at the end of the previous reaction. Stirring was stopped when colloidal agglomerates appeared in the system, and the system was left to settle for 30 min. Then, 70 mL of the settled supernatant was removed, and 3.37 mg of CQD@ 5 was added to the remaining 10 mL of the turbid solution. This operation was followed by thorough stirring until the settled colloidal particles were again dispersed steadily. The above re-stabilized and dispersed colloidal solution was then diluted to 44.2 mL. Then, 20 mL of the diluted solution was taken and mixed with the contaminants. The mixed solution was stirred well and diluted to 80 mL for the next cycle.

The concentration of CQDs was quantified by UV spectrophotometry (Text S2). The content of oxygen-containing functional groups on the surface of the CQDs was determined by Boehm titration (Text S3). The pK a of the CQDs system was measured by acid-alkali titration[29] (Text S4). Details of the electron paramagnetic resonance were given in Text S5. Organic concentrations were determined by high-performance liquid chromatography on a C18 column (4.6×150 mm) under the analytical conditions described in Table S1.

UV spectrophotometry was used to quantify the concentration of the CQDs ( Fig. 1a, S1, and S2). As shown in Fig. 1a, the yields of CQD@ 1 and CQD@ 5 increased by 22.69% and 49.50%, respectively, compared to CQD@ 0. However, when the amount of hydrogen peroxide in the electrolyte rose to 10 mM, the yield of CQD@ 10 reduced to 88.45% of that of CQD@ 0. This phenomenon is because a suitable amount of H2O2 can be activated to·OH at the cathode during electrolysis, which is favorable to the preparation of CQDs[35]. However, when the amount of H2O2 was excessive, the self-quenching of·OH was prompted in the electrolyte [15]. The decrease in·OH content due to large amounts of·OH self-quenching is detrimental to the production of CQDs, and this phenomenon occurs when the H2O2 concentration exceeds 8 mM (Fig. S3 and S4).

TEM was carried out to investigate the morphology of CQDs. As shown in Fig. 1b, the mean particle sizes of all were less than 5 nm and uniformly dispersed. Furthermore, the mean particle size of the CQDs obtained by employing H2O2 solution as the electrolyte was smaller than that of CQDs@ 0. The particle size of CQDs@ 1 was the smallest of all, at 1.75 nm. The results above indicated that employing H2O2 solution as an electrolyte can enhance the electrochemical exfoliation of CQDs.[36] Additionally, the lattice spacing of CQDs (Fig. S5) was the same as graphite 100 crystal plane spacing at 0.21 nm [20,27,37].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Defect level and functional groups of CQDs
ce:section.ce:para: To determine the defect level and functional groups of CQDs, Raman spectra, XPS, FTIR, and Boehm titration of CQDs were recorded individually. As shown in Fig. 1c, there were two significant peaks at 1350 cm−1 and 1620 cm−1 in the Raman spectra, which correspond to the D-band and G-band of the carbon materials, respectively. The G-band is associated with sp2 hybridized carbon, while the D-band is attributed to disordered carbon[38]. The defect level of carbon materials can be determined from the intensity ratio of bands D and G [29]. It can be seen that the ID/IG ratio of the CQDs increased from 0.64 to 1.02 and then slightly decreased to 0.97 as the concentration of H2O2 in the electrolyte rose from 0 to 10 mM. This result implied that hydrogen peroxide in the electrolyte could enrich the flaws in the CQDs.[36] When the concentration of H2O2 in the electrolyte reached 5 mM, the defect level of CQDs peaked.

XPS was employed to determine the elemental characteristics of CQDs. The C 1 s profile of CQDs could be deconvoluted into three peaks at 284.8, 286.8, and 288.8 eV (Fig. 1d), corresponding to C-sp2, C-O, and CO, respectively [39]. The deconvolution results of the different CQDs indicated that CQD@ 5 had the lowest content of C-sp2, which meant that the oxidation degree of CQD@ 5 was the highest. The C-sp2 contents of the CQD@ 0, CQD@ 1, and CQD@ 10 were 59.76%, 55.78%, and 53.27%, respectively, which agreed well with the flaw level determined by Raman spectra.

To further determine the functional groups on the surface of CQDs, the FTIR spectra of all CQDs were recorded. As shown in Fig. S6, FTIR exhibited three intense peaks at 1691 cm−1, 1573 cm−1, and 1220 cm−1, which corresponded to the stretching vibration of CO, the bending vibration of CC[40], and the stretching vibration of C-O[20]. It can be seen that the peak intensities of CQD@ 1, CQD@ 5, and CQD@ 10 at 1691 and 1220 cm−1 were stronger than CQD@ 0, and the peak intensities at 1573 cm−1 reversed. This result was consistent with the XPS, which indicated that H2O2 can improve the oxidation degree of CQDs. Then, the Boehm titration [29] was carried out to quantify the oxygen-containing functional groups of CQDs. The results are depicted in Fig. 1e. It can be seen that the total content of oxygen-containing functional groups (hydroxyl, carboxyl, and lactone) in the CQDs increased when hydrogen peroxide was present in the electrolyte. This result demonstrated that H2O2 improved the number of functional groups on the CQDs. The carboxyl (-COOH) contents were 11.25, 17.50, 22.66, and 16.56 mmol/g, corresponding to CQD@ 0, CQD@ 1, CQD@ 5, and CQD@ 10, respectively. It is obvious that the CQDs prepared in H2O2 solutions are rich in carboxyl. The most significant -COOH content was determined on the CQD@ 5. When the concentration of H2O2 in the electrolyte rose to 10 mM, part of the -COOH was mineralized by·OH produced from excessive H2O2, resulting in a reduction in carboxyl.

The carboxyl on the surface of CQDs can dissociate or bind with protons when the acidic and basic conditions of the solution vary, exhibiting a capacity for buffering[29]. When the pH of a system is equal to its pK a, the buffering capacity is the greatest at a given concentration of buffer pairs. According to the acid-base titration (Text S4), the pK a of different CQDs was determined. As shown in Fig. 1f and Fig. S7, the pK a of CQDs produced with H2O2 as the electrolyte was more prominent than that of CQDs@ 0. The pK a of CQD@ 5 at 5.34 was the highest among all, which meant acid-base stability could be maintained over a higher pH (pK a ± 1).

In conclusion, the yield and carboxyl content of CQDs can be effectively regulated by electrochemical exfoliation of graphite rods in H2O2 solutions. The yield and carboxyl content of optimal CQD@ 5 were 1.49 and 2.01 times those of CQD@ 0, respectively. In addition, the buffering pH of CQD@ 5 is the highest.

Subsequently, the CQDs prepared were mixed with Fe(III). The UV spectra, XPS, Raman spectra, and potentiometric titrations were collected to clarify the relation between CQDs and Fe(III) and the differences among various CQDs-Fe(III) systems. The UV spectra of the CQD@ 5-Fe(III) system are depicted in Fig. 2a. It can be seen that the UV absorption peak shifted towards the long wavelength as the Fe(III) concentration increased. This phenomenon suggested a decrease in system energy after adding Fe(III) to the CQD@ 5 solution, which could be caused by the generation of chemical bonds between Fe(III) and CQD@ 5. In addition, the binding energy of both C 1 s and O 1 s of CQDs increased (Fig. 2b) after mixing with Fe(III), which indicated that the electron cloud density on the surface of CQDs decreased[41]. It is attributed to the migration of the electron cloud from the CQDs to the Fe center[29]. The binding-energy difference values of carbon elements before and after mixing with Fe(III) (Δ Binding Energy of C) were 0.04, 0.08, 0.10, and 0.06 eV, corresponding to CQD@ 0, CQD@ 1, CQD@ 5, and CQD@ 10, respectively. Accordingly, the Δ Binding Energy of O were 0.13, 0.25, 0.26, and 0.23 eV. Both the Δ Binding Energy of C and the Δ Binding Energy of O of the CQD@ 5 were the largest among the four groups of CQDs, indicating the most intense electron shift from the CQD@ 5 to the Fe center.

To further clarify whether the chemical bond appeared between Fe(III) and CQD@ 5, the FT-IR spectra of CQD@ 5-Fe(III) were recorded. As shown in Fig. 2c, the three peaks at 1691 cm−1,1573 cm−1, and 1220 cm−1 correspond to the stretching vibration of CO, the bending vibration of CC, and the stretching vibration of C-O. The intensities of the above three peaks did not show any significant difference when CQDs were complexed with different amounts of Fe(III). It is noteworthy that relative to the FT-IR spectra of CQD@ 5, the FT-IR spectra of CQD@ 5 mixed with Fe(III) show a significant absorption at 1350 cm−1 and further enhanced with the increase of Fe(III) content. The peak at 1425 cm−1 is attributable to δ-OH of -COOH[28]. After complexation with Fe(III), the vibrational frequency of the δ-OH of -COOH decreases, and the absorption peak shifts towards the low wave number to 1350 cm−1. Therefore, the absorption located at 1350 cm−1 is the characteristic absorption of COO-Fe, and its absorption intensity is enhanced with the degree of complexation of CQDs with Fe(III). This conclusion is further confirmed by the Raman spectrum (Fig. S8) and the decrease of carboxyl groups on the surface of CQDs after the addition of Fe(III) to the CQDs (Fig. 2d). As shown in Fig. 2d, introducing Fe(III) to CQDs resulted in a reduction in the -COOH content (Δ -COOH). The remarkable reduction in carboxyl was measured in CQD@ 5 at 15.37 mmol/g after complexation with 12 μM Fe(III). The carboxyl on the surface of CQD@ 0 decreased the slightest to 8.90 mmol/g. The trend of Δ -COOH for various CQDs agreed well with that of Δ Binding Energy of C and Δ Binding Energy of O. In other words, the more carboxyls involved in the complexation with Fe(III), the more significant the decrease of electron density on the surface of CQDs. This phenomenon is because the Fe(III) coordinating with the carboxyl acts as a hole doping process, which would result in the electron transfer from electron-rich CQDs to Fe(III) through the -COO-Fe bond [29]. The more carboxyl of CQDs complexed with Fe, the more electrons are transferred from CQDs to Fe(III). Accordingly, the electron density of CQDs decreased significantly. The number of carboxyl groups on CQD@ 5 complexed with Fe(III) was 1.73 times that of CQD@ 0, which means a more intense electron transfer from CQD@ 5 to Fe(III).

ce:section.ce:section-title: CQDs-Fe(III)/H2O2 systems for the degradation of pollutants
ce:section.ce:para: To identify the performance of different CQDs-Fe(III), they were applied to the CQDs-Fe(III)/H2O2 system for phenol degradation. As shown in Fig. 3a, the phenol removals were 93.43%, 77.48%, 71.45%, and 65.10% in the CQDs-Fe(III)/H2O2 systems using CQD@ 5, CQD@ 1, CQD@ 10, and CQD@ 0, respectively. Their reaction kinetic constants were 0.0416, 0.0245, 0.0213, and 0.0176 min−1 respectively (Fig. S9). It can be seen that the CQDs-Fe(III)/H2O2 system using CQDs prepared in H2O2 solutions did better in phenol degradation than the CQD@ 0-Fe(III)/H2O2 system. The systems with CQD@ 5 exhibited the most excellent performance of all, with a reaction kinetic constant 2.36-fold that of the system using CQD@ 0. Under the same conditions, the phenol degradation performances of control systems are depicted in Fig. 3b. It can be observed that the CQDs/H2O2 system exhibited almost no phenol degradation, which indicated that the CQDs alone had a negligible effect on the hydrogen peroxide decomposition. Additionally, due to the sluggish Fe(III)/Fe(II) cycle impeded by the hydrolysis of iron ions, there was little phenol degradation in Fe(III)/H2O2 and Fe(II)/H2O2 systems. However, CQDs can effectively prevent the hydrolysis of iron ions and accelerate the Fe(III)/Fe(II) cycle via electron transfer [29]. Hence, excellent phenol degradation can be realized in the CQDs-Fe(III)/H2O2 systems.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The mechanics for the degradation-performance improvement
ce:section.ce:para: As shown in Fig. 3a, there were differences in phenol degradation among CQDs-Fe(III)/H2O2 systems using different CQDs. In order to clarify the reasons for their performance discrepancies, ESR, XPS, and linear fitting were recorded. Above all, quenching, radical trapping, and semi-quantitative experiments were employed to find the reactive oxygen species (ROS) in the CQDs-Fe(III)/H2O2 system. As shown in Fig. S10,·OH is the ROS for phenol degradation, which agrees with the existing study[29]. Therefore, the varied·OH content may be responsible for different phenol degradation performances in different systems, as demonstrated by DMPO-·OH signals from the four systems. As shown in Fig. 4a, the signal intensity agreed well with the phenol degradation performance---The more intense the DMPO-·OH signal, the higher the phenol removal.

In the CQDs-Fe(III)/H2O2 process, the·OH was produced from H2O2 decomposition catalyzed by CQDs-Fe(II)[29]. Therefore, Fe(II) content differences may be the root cause of differences in phenol degradation. The XPS spectra of the four systems were collected to determine the Fe(II) content differences among the four systems. As shown in Fig. 4b, the profile of the Fe 2p region was deconvoluted into two broad peaks corresponding to Fe 2p 3/2, Fe 2p 1/2, and their shake-up satellites. The binding energies at 711.34 eV and 723.82 eV corresponded to Fe(II), while 714.55 eV and 726.58 eV related to Fe(III) [42]. As shown in Fig. 4c, the Fe(II) to total iron (Fe(II)/Fe(Total)) ratios of the four CQDs-Fe(III)/H2O2 systems differ. The Fe(II)/Fe(Total) ratio decreased in the order of CQD@ 5, CQD@ 1, CQD@ 10, and CQD@ 0-Fe(III). The highest Fe(II)/Fe(Total) ratio was determined from CQD@ 5-Fe(III) at 68.20%, and the smallest was 61.04% in CQD@ 0-Fe(III). It can be noticed that the groups with the best phenol degradation performance had the largest Fe(II)/Fe(Total) ratio and vice versa. In order to further investigate the relationship between the Fe(II) content and the phenol degradation performance, the Fe(II)/Fe(Total) ratios in the four CQDs-Fe(III)/H2O2 systems were linearly fitted to their kinetic constants for phenol degradation. As shown in Fig. S11, a robust linear relationship was evident in the result (R2 =0.9736), which implies that the Fe(II)/Fe(Total) ratio is the key to phenol degradation in different systems.

Fe(II) content in the CQDs-Fe(III)/H2O2 system was affected by the Fe(III)/Fe(II) cycle. The faster the Fe(III)/Fe(II) cycle, the bigger the Fe(II)/Fe(Total) ratio. The former can be effectively enhanced by the through-bond electron transfer involved by carboxyl in the CQDs-Fe(III)/H2O2 system [29]. According to the titration result in Section 3.2, the amount of carboxyl engaged in complexation (Δ -COOH) varied across the different CQDs-Fe(III) systems (Fig. 2d). Meanwhile, the degree of through-bond electron transfer varied in different systems, which can be reflected on the binding energy decrease of Fe(Δ binding energy of Fe) after the CQDs-Fe(III)/H2O2 reaction (Fig. 4b)[41,43]. In order to elucidate the relationship between the carboxyl complexed with Fe(III) and the degree of electron transfer, a liner fitting between Δ -COOH and Δ binding energy of Fe was made. As shown in Fig. 4d, the result exhibited an intense linear relationship (R2 =0.9821), which implied that more intense electron transfer occurred due to more carboxyl complexing with Fe(III).

Based on the above analyses, it can be seen that the different amounts of carboxyl engaged in complexation with Fe(III) resulted in the discrepancy in the Fe(III)/Fe(II) cycle. The more carboxyl groups involved in complexation, the faster the Fe(III)/Fe(II) cycle. Accordingly, the larger the Fe(II)/Fe(Total), the more efficient the decomposition of H2O2 to produce·OH. This chain of processes ultimately determines the degradation efficiency of phenol.

Furthermore, the degradation performances of different systems were recorded at pH= 4–9. As shown in Fig. 5, S12, and S13, both the Fe(II)/H2O2 and the Fe(III)/H2O2 systems can degrade phenol effectively at pH= 4. Nevertheless, at pH ≥ 5, the CQDs-Fe(III)/H2O2 system showed a significant advantage in phenol degradation. It was because iron ions were prone to hydrolysis under high pH conditions, hence inefficient in catalyzing the decomposition of H2O2 to produce·OH[44]. However, CQDs can effectively inhibit the hydrolysis of iron ions and accelerate the Fe(III)/Fe(II) cycle[29], which strengthens the decomposition of H2O2 to degrade the contaminants at higher pH conditions.

Furthermore, the CQD@ 5-Fe(III)/H2O2 system exhibited better degradation performance than all the remaining CQDs- Fe(III)/H2O2 systems under the same pH conditions. The reaction kinetic constants of CQD@ 5-Fe(III)/H2O2 at pH= 4–9 were 1.36, 1.55, 2.36, 9.90, 10.13, and 1.08-fold those of CQD@ 0-Fe(III)/H2O2, respectively (Fig. S14). Even at pH= 8, 41.24% of phenol can be degraded with a reaction kinetic constant of 0.0083 min−1, which is 7.77 and 10.38 times that of CQD@ 0-Fe(III)/H2O2, respectively. In addition to the increased Fe(II)/Fe(Total) ratio due to a stronger electron transfer, the higher pK a of CQD@ 5 enhanced the oxidative capacity of·OH at higher pH, reinforcing the degradation performance of phenol. However, the degradation of phenol was negligible at pH ≥ 9. This phenomenon was caused by the massive hydrolysis of iron ions when the acidity of the system exceeded the buffer range of CQDs. In conclusion, the CQDs-Fe(III)/H2O2 system was superior to the traditional Fenton and Fenton-like procedures in phenol degradation at pH ≥ 4. The phenol degradation performance was also enhanced in the CQDs-Fe(III)/H2O2 system using CQDs prepared in H2O2 solution at a wide pH range, especially for the CQD@ 5-Fe(III)/H2O2 system.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The effect of the reaction parameter
ce:section.ce:para: Subsequently, the effect of Fe(III), CQDs, and H2O2 amounts on the degradation of phenol in the CQD@ 5-Fe(III)/H2O2 system was examined individually (Fig. S15 and Fig. S16).

The effect of Fe(III) concentration was investigated first. As shown in Fig. 6a, the degradation performance was optimal when the concentration of Fe(III) rose to 12 μM, with a phenol removal of 93.43% and a reaction kinetic constant of 0.0416 min−1. Insufficient iron was ineffective in catalyzing the decomposition of H2O2, resulting in poor phenol degradation. When the concentration of iron ions was excessive, the zeta potential of the system decreased, and agglomeration of the CQD@ 5-Fe occurred [29]. The cluster of catalysts inhibited their catalytic activity, thus hampering the phenol degradation. Accordingly, 12 μM was the optimum Fe(III) concentration.

The influence of CQD@ 5 concentration on phenol degradation was then investigated. As shown in Fig. 6b, the phenol removal increased and subsequently declined as the concentration of CQD@ 5 increased from 4.5 to 22 and then to 36 mg/L, and the peak appeared at CQD@ 5 = 22 mg/L. When the concentration of CQD@ 5 increased from 4.5 mg/L to 22 mg/L, the carboxyl involved in complexation with Fe(III) rose, resulting in a faster Fe(III)/Fe(II) cycle. Therefore, the phenol removal increased from 1.23% to 93.43%. In addition, the solution became more buffered with the increasing concentration of CQD@ 5. Both of these resulted in increasing phenol removal. When the concentration of CQD@ 5 surpassed 22 mg/L, the electron-rich CQD@ 5 quenched the·OH produced in the reaction[26], reducing the phenol degradation efficiency to 83.78%. Therefore, the optimum concentration of CQDs@ 5 was 22 mg/L.

Finally, the effect of H2O2 amount on the phenol degradation performances in CQD@ 5-Fe(III)/H2O2 systems was examined. According to the stoichiometric ratio, 14 mol of H2O2 is required to completely mineralize 1 mol of phenol (C6H6O+14 H2O2→6 CO2 +17 H2O). Here, four concentrations of hydrogen peroxide at 1.6, 3.2, 6.4, and 10 mM were selected, corresponding to 0.9, 1.8, 3.5, and 5.5 times the H2O2 amount required for 0.106 mM phenol mineralization, respectively. The results are shown in Fig. 6c. As the hydrogen peroxide concentration increased from 1.6 mM to 6.4 mM, H2O2 decomposed to·OH and HO2 - efficiently[45]. As a result, the phenol removal rose from 60.48% to 93.43%, and the reaction kinetic constants increased from 0.0154 to 0.0416 min−1. However, a self-quenching reaction occurred when the H2O2 concentration reached 10 mM, reducing the amount of·OH [42,46]. The removal of phenol and the reaction kinetic constant were lowered to 80.54% and 0.0265 min−1 at 10 mM of H2O2, respectively. Therefore, 6.4 mmol/L of hydrogen peroxide was determined to be the optimal concentration for phenol degradation.

The reusability and universality of catalytic systems are significant evaluation properties. Therefore, it is necessary to investigate the reusability and universality of the CQD@ 5-Fe(III)/H2O2 system. For the CQDs-Fe(III) colloidal system, the recycling experiments were realized by adding Fe(III) to the completed reaction system to recover the catalyst and adding CQDs to re-dispersed the catalyst into the new reaction system (Fig. S17 and S18). The results of the cycling experiment are shown in Fig. 7a. It can be seen that the removal of phenol retained 89.63% after six cycles, which was 0.96-fold that of the first cycle, exhibiting excellent stability for reuse. The quinones produced in the previous reaction had a positive effect on phenol degradation[29,47]. Meanwhile, the degradation performances of the CQD@ 5-Fe(III)/H2O2 system for other organics were investigated (Fig. 7b and S19). As a benzene derivative, 4-CP is an essential precursor for synthesizing drugs and dyes. BPA is an environmental hormone composed of two phenols linked to propane. Both of the two were selected as the target pollutants to evaluate the universality of the CQD@ 5-Fe(III)/H2O2 system. As shown in Fig. 7b, the degradation of BPA and 4-CP can reach 92.79% and 62.91% within 60 min at pH= 6. In comparison, Fe(II)/H2O2 and Fe(III)/H2O2 systems had a negligible effect on their degradation at the same condition. This result suggested a good universality of the CQD@ 5-Fe(III)/H2O2 system for pollutant degradation.

In summary, electrochemical exfoliation of graphite rods in hydrogen peroxide solution is an available way to regulate the yield and carboxyl content of CQDs. The yield and carboxyl content of the optimal CQDs (CQD@5) were 1.49 and 2.01 times those of CQDs prepared by conventional electrochemical exfoliation (CQD@0), respectively. Employing CQD@ 5 in the CQDs-Fe(III)/H2O2 system to degrade phenol, the pseudo-first-order kinetic constant (k obs) was 0.0416 min−1, which was 2.36-fold that of the system applying CQD@ 0. The promotion mechanism of CQD@ 5 to CQDs-Fe(III)/H2O2 systems was attributed to the intense electron transfer to Fe(III) through more -COO-Fe bond, which resulted in a faster Fe(III)/Fe(II) cycle and a bigger Fe(II)/Fe(Total) ratio. Therefore, a large amount·OH can be generated to degrade the phenol. In addition, the higher pK a of CQD@ 5 enhances the oxidation potential of·OH at high pH. Hence, the phenol degradation can be strengthened at pH= 4–8. Additionally, the reusability and universality of CQD@ 5-Fe(III)/H2O2 were remarkable, exhibiting promising application potential. In conclusion, a method for tuning the yield and carboxyl content of CQDs was proposed, and the mechanism for enhanced phenol degradation performance in the CQD@ 5-Fe(III)/H2O2 system was demonstrated. This study provides new insights for the application of CQDs Fenton-like systems.

Liao Liyan: Formal analysis, Methodology. Guo Jiaxin: Formal analysis, Software. Liang Jianxing: Conceptualization, Formal analysis, Methodology, Writing – review & editing. Li Jingdong: Conceptualization, Formal analysis, Methodology, Writing – review & editing. Jia Jinping: Conceptualization, Formal analysis, Methodology, Project administration, Resources, Supervision, Validation, Writing – review & editing. Li Yibo: Data curation, Formal analysis, Investigation, Methodology, Validation, Visualization, Writing – original draft.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Project No. 21737002). The authors would like to thank Mr. Kan Li for guiding the thesis ideas and Ms. Yan Zhu for her help in the experiments.

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.jece.2024.112008.
